title: Working with the part that sabotages every good thing
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
There's a part of you that destroys good things

I know this part. Maybe you know it too. It shows up right when something starts to feel real. A relationship that actually feels safe. A job that doesn't make you want to hide under your desk. A moment of genuine peace. And then, like clockwork, something inside you pulls the fire alarm. You pick a fight. You ghost someone. You quit. You sabotage yourself so thoroughly that even you can't believe you did it again.

Here's the thing. That part isn't your enemy. I know it feels like one. I've cursed mine a thousand times. But the parts that wreck our lives are almost always trying to protect us from something worse. Something that happened before we had words for it.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems therapy, calls this the "firefighter" part. It's the part that rushes in with extreme measures when a buried wound gets triggered. It doesn't care about long-term consequences. It cares about putting out the fire right now, by any means necessary. And sometimes that means burning down the whole house to stop the smoke.

Think about it. When did that part first show up? For me, it was around age six. I learned that hope was dangerous. That trusting someone meant getting hurt. That every good thing came with a hidden cost I couldn't afford. So my system developed a part whose job was to destroy good things before they could destroy me. Smart kid, right? Except now I'm an adult, and that same part is still operating on six-year-old logic.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker maps out exactly how these protective parts develop and why they keep running the show long after we've escaped the original danger.

The logic of self-sabotage makes perfect sense

Let me be clear about something. Self-sabotage isn't a character flaw. It's not a lack of willpower. It's not you being "broken" or "unworthy of good things." It's a survival strategy that your nervous system learned because it worked. Once. In a situation where you had no other options.

Let me tell you about the protector parts. The ones that look like anger, or control, or perfectionism, or emotional walls. In IFS, we call them managers and firefighters. Managers try to prevent pain by controlling everything. Firefighters try to extinguish pain after it's already been triggered - through numbing, bingeing, dissociating, raging.

I used to hate my protector parts. The part that made me cold and distant. The part that made me work until I collapsed. The part that drank too much. I thought they were the problem. Turns out they were the solution - the best solution my system could find at the time. They kept me alive when nothing else could.

Notice if there's a part of you that's skeptical right now. A part that says "this is too simple" or "this won't work for me" or "I'm too damaged for this." That's a protector too. It's trying to save you from hope, because hope has been dangerous before. It's been followed by disappointment too many times. Can you thank that part? Can you say "I get it. You're trying to protect me from getting hurt again." That's not weakness. That's the beginning of internal trust.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, showed us that the nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. When your system detects a threat, it mobilizes. Fight, flight, freeze, or fawn. But here's what most people don't understand. The nervous system also scans for opportunity. For connection. For joy. And sometimes, those things feel just as dangerous as a predator.

Stay with me here. When you grew up in an environment where good things were always taken away, where happiness was punished, where success made you a target, your system learned that visibility equals vulnerability. That feeling good is a setup for feeling worse. So when something good shows up now, your ancient survival circuits go, "Nope. We know how this ends. Let's get out before we get hurt again."

That part that sabotages? It's not stupid. It's terrified. It's doing exactly what it was trained to do. It's just that the training happened in a different world, and now it's running old code in a new environment.

I see this with clients all the time. They get a promotion and immediately start showing up late. They fall in love and suddenly find reasons to push their partner away. They start a healing practice and then abandon it the moment it starts working. The pattern is so consistent it's almost predictable. Wild, right?

Getting curious instead of fighting the part

Most of us try to beat this part into submission. We shame ourselves. We make promises we can't keep. We white-knuckle our way through life, trying to force ourselves to accept good things we don't actually feel safe receiving. And it never works. Because you can't bully a terrified part into trusting. You can only meet it with compassion.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps explain why we developed these protective parts in the first place, and why they're so stubborn about letting go.

Here's what I've learned. When the sabotage part shows up, the last thing it needs is more rejection. It needs to be heard. It needs to know that someone finally sees it, and that someone isn't going to punish it for doing its job.

I talk to this part now. Not in a weird way, but like I'd talk to a scared kid who's trying to protect me. I say things like, "I know you're trying to keep me safe. I know you've seen things fall apart before. I'm not going to ignore you. But I want to show you that this time might be different. Can we just stay with this feeling for a minute?"

I think about exile parts a lot. In IFS language, exiles are the young, wounded parts that carry the pain, the shame, the memories that the protector parts work so hard to keep locked away. They're the parts we're most afraid of. Because if we let them speak, if we let them be seen, we're afraid the pain will swallow us whole.

But here's what Richard Schwartz found in decades of clinical work. When exiles are finally witnessed - when someone (even your own Self) turns toward them with compassion instead of away from them with fear - they don't destroy you. They soften. They release the burdens they've been carrying. Sometimes for decades.

You don't have to do this alone. And you don't have to do it all at once. But I want you to know that those parts of you that feel too much, that carry too much, that seem too broken to touch - they're not too anything. They're just waiting. Waiting for someone to say "I see you. I'm not leaving. You don't have to carry this alone anymore." And sometimes, that someone can be you.

And usually, something shifts. The part relaxes, just a little. It stops screaming. It starts whispering. And in that whisper, I can hear what it's actually afraid of. Not the job. Not the relationship. But the shame of being rejected again. The pain of trusting and being betrayed. The unbearable weight of hope.

Building a relationship with the protector

This is where the real work happens. Not in eliminating the sabotage part, but in transforming your relationship with it. You become the adult in the room. The part that was forced to grow up too fast gets to be a child again, and you get to be the one who holds it.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz walks through the process of getting to know these protectors without judgment, and it's honestly kind of beautiful.

I want you to try something. Next time that sabotage part flares up, don't act on it. Don't send that text. Don't quit that job. Don't cancel that appointment. Instead, just notice. Feel the impulse in your body. Where is it? Chest? Throat? Stomach? What does it feel like? Hot? Cold? Tight? Shaky?

Then, if you can, ask it a question. Not with words, but with curiosity. "What are you afraid would happen if I stayed?" Listen for the answer. It might come as a feeling, a memory, a voice. It might say something like, "They'll leave anyway. You'll be humiliated. It's better to leave first."

Notice what just happened in your body. That's the part speaking. And now you know what it's protecting you from. Not the present moment, but a ghost from the past. A wound that's still alive inside you.

From there, you can offer comfort. You can say, "I'm here now. I'm an adult. I can handle this. You don't have to carry this alone anymore." It sounds simple. It's not. It takes practice. But it works.

Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation helped me understand something crucial. After trauma, the personality doesn't just get "damaged" - it fragments. Not in a dramatic, movie-style way. In a quiet, everyday way. The part that goes to work and smiles at colleagues. The part that cries in the shower. The part that goes numb when someone gets too close. These aren't masks. They're survival adaptations.

I lived like this for years without knowing it. Switching between states so smoothly that no one noticed. Not even me. The high-functioning part handled everything visible. The wounded parts only came out when I was alone, or drunk, or so exhausted that the walls came down. Sound familiar?

Here's what I want you to know. Those parts don't need to be eliminated. They need to be heard. They need to know that the danger is over, that they can put down the weapons they've been carrying. But they won't believe it just because you tell them. They need to feel it. In the body. In repeated experiences of safety. It takes time. It takes patience. It takes more self-compassion than most of us know how to give.

The paradox of healing the saboteur

Here's the uncomfortable truth. That part might never fully go away. And that's okay. The goal isn't to eradicate it. The goal is to make it smaller. To give it a different job. To let it rest.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who trained with Bessel van der Kolk, talks about this all the time. She says we don't get rid of parts. We help them find new roles. The part that used to sabotage relationships might become the part that helps you set boundaries. The part that made you quit jobs might become the part that knows when a situation is truly toxic. The protector becomes an advisor.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work is perfect for this stage, when you're learning to hold these parts with tenderness instead of fighting them.

I'm not going to tell you that this is easy. It's not. Some days, that part is so loud I can't hear anything else. Some days, I still sabotage things. I still ghost people. I still quit things that matter. But I'm slower to do it now. I catch myself more often. And when I do slip, I don't add shame on top of it. I just say, "Okay. That happened. What can I learn?"

That's progress. Not perfection. Just a little more space between the trigger and the reaction. A little more kindness toward the parts that are just trying to survive.

You're not broken. You're not flawed. You have a part that learned to destroy good things because good things were once dangerous. And that part can learn something new. It can learn that you're safe now. That you're strong enough to hold both the hope and the fear. That you don't have to burn down the house to survive the smoke.

Sit with that. Let it land. And then, when you're ready, start talking to that part. It's been waiting for someone to finally listen.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link)
